desc:
From here, you can clearly see a small village some two miles north and east.
This region, unlike the area to the west is level making it suitable land for
the village to come. To the north or east you can move towards the village,
while south and west leads you further away.
name: Near a Village

desc:
From here, directly behind a small village, you can see a number of small huts.
One hut appears to be larger than the others and the bright paintings on its
exterior seems to indicate that the hut holds some significance within the
village. A large fenced in corral blocks direct movement towards the north. The
plains continue to the west, south and east.
name: Behind the Village

desc:
The thick long grass forms a blanket of green stretching for many miles in all
directions. While it appears that an occasional elk or deer has wandered this
way, the grass is thick and hardy and covers their passing leaving little in the
way of a trail. An open region to the south can be seen where a water hole has
formed and beyond it and to the west a single tall tree helps to maintain your
orientation upon these plains. To the north and west some four miles a small
village takes shape.
name: Grasslands

desc:
This small hill slopes gently down to the west where it meets the surrounding
moors. To the northwest a stone pile stands near the river bank. A few small
holly bushes have sprung up from the dark rocky ground here, but they seem to be
struggling against the harsh conditions of this land.
name: Hills of Dunland

desc:
This hill is almost bare of the holly bushes and much greener than the adjacent
hills. The reason for the sudden change in vegetation is the proximity of the
hill to a nearby forested valley, which is directly east. The valley, as seen
from here, is a sparse forest, with varying hues of green vegetation. It
continues to the east and northeast, becoming denser in those directions.
name: Hills of Eregion

desc:
Here, the hills have considerably less vegetation than they do further north,
where they are dotted pink-red by the abundance of berry bearing holly bushes.
Looking outwards from here, hills can be seen to extend in all directions. To
the east, beyond the next hill, the beginning of a forested valley is faintly
visible.
name: Hills of Eregion

desc:
The hills are more rugged here than farther east, and they continue both north
and west. Small patches of woods are fairly common - although hardly a forest,
there is plenty of wood available should the need arise. At one point, a small
cave is visible in one of the hillsides.
name: Hills of Eregion

desc:
These low hills are well-worn, with few sharp faces or gulleys around to thwart
a traveler. Several of the small valleys tucked among the hills are home to
small patches of woods. Game seems plentiful, judging by myriad trails leading
through the underbrush.
name: Sloping Grassland

desc:
A large water hole provides a place for wildlife to gather during times of
thirst. While the source of the water is not clear, it is probable that this is
a low point in the plains; and therefore water collects after each heavy rain
fall. Alternatively, there could be an underground stream which provides a
steady source of clean water. On occasion a deer or elk appears to be licking
the soft mud but is more likely feasting upon a natural deposit of salt. While
the plains continue in all directions, the region is well marked with a village
some five miles to the north and a bit west, a large singular tree to the
southeast, and the waves of a rolling hills in the distant west.
name: Water Hole

desc:
An endless expanse of grass continues in all directions. While an occasional elk
or deer can be spotted, the land is mostly barren of wildlife. From here, the
faint outline of a small village can be seen towards the north, while rolls of
some distant hills slowly take shape some four to eight miles west of here. For
the thirsty, a water hole can be reached by traveling due east.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
This plain contains countless blades of tall, green grass. While a few trampled
blades indicate that wildlife or men have passed this way previously, little
else indicates their passage. While the land continues to be level in the north,
east, and south, to the west a hill slowly forms.
name: Grassy Plains of Hollin

desc:
As the hills before and after, this hill has been well-worn by the countless
years upon which it has braved the onslaught of rain, snow and wind and contains
few sharp faces or gullys to thwart a traveler. While there is not much to
distinguish this hill from the others, a single graceful tree continues to
thrive as it has for many years before this one.
name: Sloping Grassland

desc:
A low point upon the Eregion hills the view of the village further north and
east is blocked. A soft upward slope continues to the south while the terrain
levels off to the north. The backside of a cave blocks immediate movement
towards the north but it can be circumvented to the west and east.
name: Hills of Eregion

desc:
While from a distance this dip is quite visible, here, in the shallow valley,
the only immediate visible difference between this region and those to the
north, south, west and east is that the village to the north and east is no
longer visible. The upward slopes are so shallow that they would be barely
distinguishable to someone walking through these hills. The grass and bushes are
slightly richer in colour from the additional water that has seeped through the
ground.
name: Dip in Hills of Eregion

desc:
This hill is barren of the holly bushes which paint much of the landscape with
their pink-red berries. To the east there is a small dip in the hill, which,
from here would be barely visible to a traveler, but from a distance helps
create an illusion of rolling hills. To the south there is a small cave, little
more than a crevice in a small boulder deposited many years before this region
became a grassy hill.
name: Hills of Eregion

desc:
A large boulder, once the width and height of many men, appears to have split
from the bottom upwards as nature pulled the large rock apart. Over the years,
from freezing rain and natural erosion, this crevice slowly evolved into a small
cave, not much larger than a small elf. The bottom of the cave is composed of
the soft soil which forms the base of the Eregion hills, while the top is the
sharp edge of splitting granite.
name: Shallow Cave

desc:
A calm serenity fills the quiet repose under the green banks of the Glanduin
river. A joyful gurgle rises from the water where a green-leafed bush at the
southern riverbank stirs the flow of the river. The current gathers up speed as
the stream rushes over a shallow section near a bend that sends the river
further west. A sudden squirt of water followed by a darting movement that
glistens through the water suggests the presence of fish.
name: The River Glanduin

desc:
Calm flows the purling Glanduin as its ever bending course shifts through the
grass-covered plains. Every now and then, the water slips over a stone or a
boulder protruding the muddy riverbed creating a gentle fall, behind which the
bubbles make a lustrous gurgling sound. Just south of the river a twisting trail
darts along the riverside. The foreshore is buzzing with insects playing in the
rich and varied plantlife whilst enjoying the comfortable access to water.
name: The River Glanduin

desc:
The river slowly flows northwards through the green plains. The water is very
calm, especially here in the middle. The water is more turbulent near the
strands where rocks are hidden beneath the surface. A small trail follows the
south shore of the river.
name: The River Glanduin

desc:
The hills continue as before sprinkled with short grass and a few holly bushes.
From this vantage point, a small village can be seen many miles to the north and
east. While movement to the east, north and south is not impeded, a large
boulder inhibits direct movement towards the west.
name: Hills of Eregion

desc:
Grass and weeds cover the lands besides the Glanduin river in bright green. A
purling sound reaches you from the river to the south and west, but north and
east the quiet plains expand far into the horizon. Grubs and beetles enjoy the
moist soil and the cool shadow under the vegetation. On the south side of the
river rise the ruins of an old village, which is accessible via a ford.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
Fields of grass, weeds and flowers span far into the northern horizon, waving
and bowing to the wind the green vision turns into a distant memory of the sea.
A sound of purling water, coming from the south, completes the image and for a
little while one may dream of the ocean shores so far away. Clusters of blue
violets spring forth as comforting waypoints on the journey through the
intriguing flora. The joyful gurgle of the river accompanies you mile after mile
as your travel continues.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
Green fields span across the horizon with waving grass and a few scattered
bushes. A small hill stands solemnly a few feet off the level of the plains, its
gently slanting side covered in coarse grass that rattles in the wind. The view
from the top is excellent and allows you to see far north across the open plains
of southern Eregion. Far to the east lie the mountains in their stunning glory
with snow-capped peaks. A few miles to the south the Glanduin river can be seen
running west towards the Sea far away.
name: Sloping, Grassy Plains

desc:
The plains slope gently in every direction without any significant landmarks in
the immediate vicinity. Green grass, nourished by the fertile soil, grows in
dense banks everywhere, and in some places reaching heights of more than two
feet. A hawk soars high in the sky, its keen eyes searching for small rodents to
catch. Although the country is mainly flat, you observe a small elevation to the
south.
name: Sloping, Grassy Plains

desc:
The downs are vegetated mainly by grass and weeds. Only occasional bushes break
the monotone and endless sea of grass, and while rare there are also small
flowers to be found whenever the grass retreats. While fairly level, the fields
appears more hilly to the south where a ridge rise a couple of miles away.
name: Grassy Downs

desc:
Wild green grass vegetates the downs in southern Eregion. Sloping gently the
downs continues in all directions seemingly endlessly. Among the grass you spot
many different types of wild flowers and berries, especially the holly bushes
thrive well. A buzzing and humming rise from among the grass, bees are busy
gathering nectar.
name: Grassy Downs

desc:
Waist-high grass with intermittent wild flowers chracterizes the vegetation in
the green downs. Although the grass is dominant, the fecund soil provides
excellent conditions for other plants as well. Various sedges and club moss are
quite common too, especially the shorter kinds. The otherwise level ground
begins a slow climb to the east where a slope leads up a small mound.
name: Grassy Downs

desc:
A mound rises from the fertile soil. Elevated about fifteen feet from the level
of the surrounding plains it provides an excellent view of the lands north of
the Glanduin river. In the east the misty mountains rise in clear silouette
against the sky. Further south the river is seen as a clear dark line before the
plains turns into a thick forest. North and west the grass- laden plains
continue, seemingly forever.
name: Grassy Mound

desc:
This small, grass-covered ridge rises gently out of the countryside. Many small
bushes cover the area, making progress to the west difficult. Though there is a
sporadic covering of grass, few animals appear to frequent the area. The ridge
continues off to the south and west before melting back into the plains of
Dunland.
name: Ridge in the Plains

desc:
The ground slowly slopes upwards to the south and east where a narrow ridge
forms. From here a burned hut can be seen some three miles to the northwest and
another ten miles beyond the hut a small village. The ground is covered with
coarse thick grass that easily masks the passage of wildlife.
name: Sloping Hill

desc:
Upon these plains, which are littered with grass and small bushes, this singular
tree has managed to grow. Even though the tree flourishes and is often laden
with fruit, not only is this tree the only one to be seen, it appears that not
even a sapling has managed to sprout for many years. To the west and north of
here a small water hole can be seen while to the east a hill begins to form.
name: Lone Tree

desc:
The grass has gradually become shorter and more dense then the regions to the
north, west and east. In areas, the rough ground peaks through the grass forming
a harsh contrast between the bright green blades and the dark brown soil. While
a path does not exist, there are various small trails headed north towards a
large water hole and towards the east a single large tree has placed its mark
upon these plains.
name: Empty Plains of Hollin

desc:
For unknown reasons, life avoids this section of the plains. The grass appears
normal, though the tips are strangely browned and the stalks tougher then one
would expect. Nothing but empty plains can be seen in all directions, the tall
grass blocking the view for many miles. Though, to the distant west some rolling
hills begin to take shape.
name: Empty Plains

desc:
The plains here continue unblemished. The grass is long and straight, every
blade unbroken, free to swirl in the wind. To the west the plains continue as
they do to the north and south. However, towards the east, while the land
continues to be level, the region becomes barren.
name: Grassy Plains of Hollin

desc:
The flat plains form a sharp contrast to the more hilly terrain north and west.
The plains continue to the south and east but nothing of interest seems to mark
those regions. From here, little can be seen of a small village to the north.
name: Flat Plains

desc:
Upon a slope, the terrain climbs slightly to the south and west while it falls
to the north. East, the region flattens out forming a level plain. A few bushes
are loosely scattered across the hill side interwined with short grass and a few
clovers. A few scattered footprints of elks can be seen in soft ground but none
are recent.
name: Sloping Hills of Eregion

desc:
The land here is a series of low swells, rising just above the level of the
surrounding plains. Ankle-deep grass covers the ground underfoot, but the land
is hidden more by the plentiful bushes and shrubs in the area than the grass.
The ground slopes gently upwards to the north, cresting a ridge.
name: Plains beside a Ridge

desc:
The ground here is generally flat, though there are small swells to the land. A
high covering of tall grasses covers the land, rising above knee level.
Goldenrod is mixed in with the grass, creating a mixture of green and bright
yellow. Slight breezes cause the entire landscape to shudder and move, creating
a hypnotic wave-like pattern.
name: In the Tall Grass

desc:
The hills have flattened out into an endless plain. To the west and north the
rolling hills paint the landscape with their curves, while somewhere, in the
distant south, flows the River Glanduin. To the east the grass thickens, and
none of the holly bushes so commonly seen to the north are visible.
name: Plains of Hollin

desc:
This area, the middle of an indistinguishable plain, is covered with a low
coarse grass with a weed or small bush embedded from spot to spot among the
grass. In all directions the land presents few distinct landmarks, making it
difficult to get your bearings, though a small burned hut does lie to the south.
name: Plains of Hollin

desc:
To the far east, a rolling hill forms at the edge of the horizon as a precursor
to the first peaks of the Misty Mountains. South of here, beyond sight, the
river Glanduin flows and not so far to the north, some ten miles from here, the
river Sirannon can be found as it winds around an ancient road. While at times
the terrain becomes less flat, much of the surrounding, as is this area, is
covered with short grass and a few holly bushes that become sprinkled with red
berries during the warm months of spring and summer.
name: Plains of Hollin

desc:
The grassy hill forms a lazy upward slant towards the south where it meets a
plateau some three miles away. To the west the remains of a burned out hut can
be seen and five miles beyond it a water hole. To the north after many miles of
plains the river Sirannon can be found and beyond it the old Hollin Road.
name: Sloping Hill

desc:
The remains of a small hut are still visible. From the placement and age of this
hut, some seven miles from the main village, it appears to have housed an
outcast. While it is not obvious if the fire that destroyed this hut was set
intentionally or accidentally, or if it was the cause of the occupant's death,
it does appear from the various well placed trinkets that the dead occupant was
honored.
name: Burned Hut

desc:
While the land is flat and indiscrete a number of nearby landmarks makes it easy
to keep your bearings. To the west, well before the rolling hills, is a water
hole, while to the east the remains of a burned out hut are visible. To the
south a tall tree stands in defiance upon the plains while to the north the land
is undistinguishable.
name: Grasslands

desc:
This filthy cage, which smells of decaying flesh and excrement, is used by the
settlement to keep a supply of war-dogs. It is a small cramp room, with no
windows, and a grate as the east wall. The walls are scratched all around,
probably made by the anxious dogs, lusting for freedom.
name: Dog Cage

desc:
This is the entrance to the camps of some Dunlendings. Looking south, several
crudely made huts can be seen spread out here and there; all facing the Village
Circle, where meetings and general gathering take place. The Dun- lendings are a
simple primitive people. They do not trust outsiders and are quick to resort to
violence against intruders. It is often best to steer clear of them.
name: Entrance to a Village

desc:
Leather skins line the walls on all sides of the small hut, keeping out drafts
and other elements of bad weather. Through the thin cracks which formed between
the outter plank lining of the hut, a small amount of light is able to penetrate
the inside of the hut. Several beds made of leather padding and furs are crowded
together, taking up most of the room.
name: Dunlending Hut

desc:
A small clearing in the camp serves as a meeting place for the Dunlendings. In
the centre is a large fur lined chair raised on a small platform. This is the
chieftain's private throne and from here he oversees the village meetings.
Several worn-out leather mats fan out on both sides of the platform, completing
a circle and providing seats to favorite soldiers of the chieftain.
name: Village Circle

desc:
Leather skins line the walls on all sides inside the small hut, keeping out
drafts and other elements of bad weather. Through thin cracks, formed between
the outer plank lining of the hut, a small amount of daylight is allowed to
penetrate the inside. Several beds, made of leather padding and furs, are
crowded together taking up most of the room.
name: Dunlending Hut

desc:
A large circular fireplace made of stones stands here in the open. Several pots
are littered around the fireplace with remains of rabbit soup and roasted deer
meat still inside. Large pieces of logs and slabs of dried cow dung are piled up
near, ready to be fed into the hearth.
name: Central Hearth

desc:
This good sized stall is the home of the most prized possesion of the Chieftain.
It is large enough to almost provide running room for the beautiful steed that
occupies it. The chieftain could not stand to have his favorite pet housed with
the common horses of the clan, so he ordered this stall ... many sizes bigger
than his own hut in fact ... to be built. Two double locked gates to the East
and West allow entrance to this cleanly scrubbed stall.
name: Private Corral

desc:
This medium sized enclosure serves as a stable for several horses. Each horse
has its own individual stall, with a fresh water trough standing in front.
Stacks of hay are line along northern wall of the pen, reaching almost all the
way to the plank covered roof. A small double locked gate stands at the east
wall. From here there is no way see what lies beyond the locked gate.
name: Animal Pen

desc:
Leather skins line the inside walls of the small hut, keeping out drafts and
other elements of bad weather. Through thin cracks formed between the outter
plank lining of the hut, a small amount of daylight is allowed to penetrate
inside. Several beds made of leather padding and furs are crowded together,
taking up most of the room.
name: Dunlending Hut

desc:
This is the entrance of a seldom visited holly cave. The room is poorly lit by
torches which are placed along the walls every few meters. The walls, blackened
by the burning torches, have taken a dark grey hue, while the floor resembles a
cobbled walkway. To the east, a cell door, which contains a small peephole, can
be seen. The corridor continues to the north where it widens out and forms a
larger cave.
name: Secret Corridor

desc:
This is the entrance of a seldom used magic cave. The walls of this place are
also dark grey in colour, but in several places painted motives have been drawn
in red pigments. A faint breeze can be felt coming from another room to the
south. The corridor continues to the north where it widens out and forms a cave,
from which a strange religious feeling and an atmosphere of respect originates.
name: Painted Corridor

desc:
Crude paintings cover the walls of the cave. They show the wild beasts hunted by
the Dunlendings for either glory or food. Most of the paintings show the hunts
of oxen, but they also show hunts of wolves, deer and bears. The main figure in
one of the paintings is a large dark bear. The paintings are drawn using
pigments such as coal and red argillite, taking advantage of the relief of the
wall's surface. Following the lines of a protuberance in the wall, the mark of
red hands can be seen just above some scattered human bones. At the foot of the
wall, next to the bones, is a stone bowl which still contains the blood of a
sacrificed prisoner.
name: Painted Cave

desc:
This is the prison cell where the Dunlendings keep those unfortunate travelers
which have been captured. It is a dark and musky room, poorly light by a single
torch provided near the door. There is a crude bed against one of the walls, and
scattered litter across the floor, probably from former prisoners.
name: Prison Cell

desc:
The trail follows the river closely, through the fertile grass on the riverbank.
Some large bushes grow alongside with reeds by the river and a very old willow
tree extend its branches over the water. Tracks in the damp earth near the river
edge suggest that this becomes a crowded place when animals become thirsty.
name: Trail along the Glanduin

desc:
Fields coloured in green and yellow border to the banks of the Glanduin River. A
little east of here the river makes a slow turn north and the low banks are
partially flooded. Reeds, bulrush and bog moss grow in the aqueous soil. Many
insects live in the moist environment, and a steady buzzing and humming can be
heard. A cosy trail bends along the riverside on a small rise of worn earth.
name: Trail along the Glanduin

desc:
The clear water, that began its journey many miles away in the snow-covered
mountain peaks to the east, trickles gently along, slowly gathering up speed to
reach the Sea. The green banks along the river are filled with plenty of
wildlife; insects buzzing, birds chirping with joy and silver fins carving
through the water below.
name: The River Glanduin

desc:
Following the contours of the river the trail continues through the grass
covered plains. The trail bends and slopes a little, yet always manages to find
its way back to the river. It often takes a minor sway away from the river to
avoid the soggy and muddy area close to the banks. The path is clear and small
pebbles makes it easy to follow.
name: Trail along the Glanduin

desc:
A pebbled trail snakes through the waist-high grass along the banks of the
Glanduin river. Once the fields south of the river were fallow, tended by the
hard-working and diligent citizens of Cillien. The rural plains are now
overgrown with wild grass, and only the trail remains to indicate the amount of
traffic that once passed south of the river. To the east can be seen the silent
ruins of the village of Cillien.
name: Trail along the Glanduin

desc:
Located on a small rise on the western bank of the Glanduin lies the ruins of a
once prosperous village. Standing in the old village square that allows a good
view east, it's possible to see far upstreams the Glanduin. Around the square
stand the ruined masonry of the houses that once formed the village. Although
crude, a decent shelter from the elements can be found among the lichen-covered
stone walls. A dusty trail runs through the village going west into the
wilderness or south into Dunland. From the north comes the babbling sound of a
ford in the river.
name: Ruins of a Village

desc:
A dusty trail runs along the western shore of the Glanduin. The river goes
north-south here as it passes on the eastern side of a ruined village to the
north. A richly vegetated slope lies between the trail and the river. The slope
is fairly steep and the plants grow too dense to see if the ground is stable
enough not to make the descend dangerous.
name: Dunland Trail

desc:
In the regions south of the Glanduin river begins the vast plains of Dunland,
through which this small path runs. Located near the Misty Mountains the region
prospers from the melting water brought down from the snowcapped heights by the
many brooks and streams. Green grass grows dense and in some places unrivalled
by plants and trees. The view of the swaying grass would be unbroken, if not for
the rising mountain about a half day's march to the south.
name: Dunlending Trail

desc:
Halfway hidden among knee-high grass and green bushes a trail snakes its way
across the Dunland plains. Once sighted, gravel and stamped dirt makes following
the trail easy. However it's not uncommon to see the vegetation overtaking the
trail trying to confuse weary travelers.
name: Dunland Trail

desc:
Stones and pebbles mark a trail well hidden among the waist-high grass that
dominates the open plains. The trail, worn and bumpy, is a welcome guide through
the monotonous landscape, where the variation in the vegetation relies only on
the shifting colour of the grass. Yet a keen eye might spot the unexpected
appearance of trefoil and crowfoot hiding among the thick grass along the side
of the trail.
name: Dunland Trail

desc:
Green grass grows on both sides of a trail that cross the flat plain. The small
bump of a hill in the north-west is the only thing breaking the straight line
formed by the border of the green grass and the colour of the sky far away near
the horizon.
name: Dunland Trail

desc:
The plain is completely flat here and covered with green grass. Different kinds
of bushes and flowers grow in the fertile soil between grassy patches. The trail
bends around brushy spots but is otherwise very straight. The dry carcass of a
horse lies beside the trail spreading an odour of decomposing flesh.
name: Dunland Trail

desc:
The water rushes forth over stones and rocks stirred by a sudden shallowness of
the river. The banks have grown further apart making the river expand to more
than a hundred feet across. Nevertheless the shallow waters are easily passed if
wet feet are no concern. On either side of the ford grows green grass and frail
bracken in comfortable distance to the cold gushing water. A bit upstream, to
the east, the river can be seen turning south around a small rise.
name: Ford in the River Glanduin

desc:
The mighty Glanduin makes a narrow bend around a cliff to the southwest. While
the plains in the borderland of Eregion and Dunland are generally void of
obstacles, this cliff is large enough to have altered the course of the river
significantly. On the low riverbanks to the north and the east is a dense growth
of bracken and weeds, obviously thriving in the moist soil. Behind the cliff the
river widens, and makes a gushing sound as the water rushes over a ford.
name: The River Glanduin

desc:
Turning north the river continues along a densely vegetated slope to the west.
Green bushes grow on the slope, and at the point where the slope makes a last
steep descent grows a thick foliage of vines that hang into the water. In the
clear water it's possible to spot many details of the hydrophytes that grow
beneath the surface, especially the comb-shaped leaves of the water-yarrow is
seen clearly. South lies a quiet meadow with soft grass.
name: The River Glanduin

desc:
On its west bound journey, through the borderland between Eregion and Dunland,
the river has reached more open land after many miles of company from a forest
on the southernly side. Now grassy fields open on either side while the banks
grow ever more green with goutweed and bracken. A small stream joins the river
from the south, and after a hectic but uneventful merging, the river flows on as
if nothing happened.
name: The River Glanduin

desc:
As the gently flowing river passes north of a coniferous forest the current is
slowly increasing. Dangerous boulders just beneath or above the water surface
create local gushes and strong currents as the river is forced around them. The
low banks on either side of the river are coloured in green by tall grass and
sporadic brackens. On the north side of the river lie open plains as far as the
eye can see.
name: The River Glanduin

desc:
The soft flow of the clear water takes the Glanduin river further west on its
journey towards its outlet in the great sea far away. The low banks of the river
are vegetated by green grass and soft brackens and many insects enjoy the moist
conditions close to the water. South of the river is a pine forest and north the
vast plains of Dunland disappear into the horizon.
name: The River Glanduin

desc:
The waters of the Glanduin river continues its mellow flow towards the great sea
far in the west. Passing north of a dead pine forest the river marks the
transition from plains to softwood. The sad sight of the withering conifers to
the south is lessened by a green light in the distance suggesting a more healthy
forest further south.
name: The River Glanduin

desc:
Purling west the river passes south of what looks to be a small settlement in
the plains north of the river. A small landing, in strong brown oak-timber,
creates a good spot for landing fish and other waterborne goods. Near the bank
the landing is elevated almost ten feet from the water surface, and it's
possible to manoeuvre a vessel under the landing. South of the river lies a
coniferous forest, which from a distance looks healthy, but a closer look
reveals that many of the trees are withered and almost dead.
name: The River Glanduin

desc:
While heading west the river comes upon a section where a low island of sharp
rocks makes the water foam as it gushes over the stones. The situation becomes
suddenly dangerous as the current steers you right towards the sharp teeth, but
at the very last moment a strong side current shifts you north and out of
danger. Once in safety you notice how the passing landscape is much different
north of the river compared to south of the river. Wide open fields, almost as
level as a dinner table, stretch far into the north, while a tired and dead
looking forest raises its crowns despondently to the south.
name: The River Glanduin

desc:
Coarse grass covers the fields north of the river in green. The country slopes
slightly upward to the north, but is otherwise quite level. The Glanduin river
can be heard clearly from the south, as a steady sound of water whisked to foam
by sharp rocks. Across the river lies a forest, but the feature in the landscape
is some buildings to the west.
name: Grassy Downs

desc:
A small hillock covered with green grass and dandelions provides a good view of
the surrounding areas. Beneath the delicate grass and flowers the soil is moist
and fecund, yet stones lie scattered just beneath the surface as if a stone
structure once stood here. Far to the south a dark forest can be seen on the
other side of a river running west.
name: Grassy Hill

desc:
Broad oak planks creaks beneath your feet as you step unto the wooden landing.
The landing is about ten feet wide and long enough to reach waters so deep that
a small boat may dock without running ashore. Resting on thick wooden stakes the
landing can support a heavy weight and in previous days it provided the
halflings villagers with an easy access to the river. A hatch appears in the
middle of the walkway, presumably used when unloading heavy cargo from boats
able to sail beneath the dock.
name: Wooden Landing

desc:
Where the plains of Eregion meet the banks of the Glanduin was once a small
village. Halflings proficient with the use of dinghys made a home here close to
the river. Fish and crayfish were plenty and provided food to support a fairly
large population. Now the halflings are gone, and only the rundown ruins of the
village remain as evidence of their presence. A dock at the bank of the river,
once used to land the fishermens catch, is still useable.
name: Ancient Settlement

desc:
In southern Eregion the fauna is reigned by green grass and wild flowers. A
tempestuous wind roams unhindered in the shelterless downs. While the vegetation
appears unvaried and dull, a careful exploration reveals that beneath the grass
grows numerous kinds of flowers; butterworth, scabious and broad-leafed cotton
grass grows quite well in these conditions.
name: Grassy Downs

desc:
Wild grass and heather dominate the open plains north the Glanduin river.
Contrary to the unconstrained trek across the downs, the terrain south of the
river would appear less manageable as a dense forest can be spotted even from
here. Far beyond the forest lies the mountains with snow-capped peaks.
name: Grassy Downs

desc:
The ridge, that runs for half a dozen miles along the northern banks of the
Glanduin river, is vegetated from top to toe by thick grass, coarse as hay.
Walking along the top of the ridge is easy, as the grass becomes thinner at the
top and the top itself being fairly wide. Beneath a ridge to the north lie open
plains that reach far into the horizon and from the south your eye catches the
gleaming reflections of a river.
name: Wide, Grassy Ridge

desc:
This grass-covered ridge gives a good view of the river that runs by to the
south. The green vegetation almost reaches your hip, and passing through the
country is not an easy task. A sudden opening in the vegetation turns out to be
a dry spot with sharp stones and rubble, and in the centre stands a big grey
stone. Standing on the ridge the whole river delta, the plains, a forest to the
east and the mountain seems to become apparent and clear as if some magic of old
clinged to this place refusing to let go.
name: Grassy Ridge

desc:
A small hill rises from the fertile soil. On the slanting hillsides is a dense
growth of coarse grass that rustles with life. Undoubtedly there are many small
animals living among the grass, right now eyeing the visitor on the hilltop. The
southern hillside is steeper than the other, and it leads right down to the bank
of the river. To the north you spot another small hill only a few miles away,
but otherwise the country is flat right up to the mountains that lie far in the
east.
name: Grassy Hill Overlooking the River

desc:
The green fields to the north of the river slope gently into the distance. There
are no trees or bushes to hinder the journey, yet the green grass and weeds grow
dense enough to encumber a tired traveler. Hiding among the vegetion are small
clusters of hogweed, easily identified by the saw-tooth patterned leaves and
thumb-thick green stems. Both to the north and the south the ground climbs a
little to reach the top of two small hills in each direction. To the west the
river flows gently by, while making a turn that takes it further west.
name: Sloping, Grassy Plains

desc:
A mixture of wild grass and weeds grows throughout the sloping plains. Small
grasstops dot the country and travelling the slanting inclines is a moderate
strain to your endurance. A grass-covered ridge lies to the south obstructing
the view, but to the north are open plains that continue seemingly endless into
the far north.
name: Widely Sloping Plains

desc:
The terrain in the grass-covered plains of southern Eregion is mainly flat, but
you have come upon a region where the land is somewhat hilly. Travelling up and
down the slopes is a tiresome exercise, but as chances are there are plenty of
suitable spots in the shelter of the small hills to make a camp. The vegetation
is uniform, consisting mainly of grass, that varies only a little in thickness
and in colour.
name: Widely Sloping Plains

desc:
A perpetual view of green grass is offered to travellers passing through the
plains of southern Eregion. The vegetation is mainly unvaried, yet erratic
clusters of wild flowers can be found here and there. In the far east and south
the lies the misty mountains, and before the mountains lies a dark forest.
name: Grassy Downs

desc:
Once serving as food storage, the old cellar is well-equipped with shelves and
cabinets lined along the walls. However, the cellar must have been sealed off
long ago judging by the thick dust layer on the floor and the grey cobwebs spun
in every corner. Rusted screw eyes in the eastern wall hold in place the remains
of a rottened staircase. Rainwater has entered the cellar through the tiny
chinks of a hole blocked by a large and very heavy stone slab. A narrow corridor
leads to the south.
name: Old Pantry

desc:
A quiet meadow, with soft grass and a wide variety of wild flowers in the peak
of their bloom, lies well-hidden just south of a scenic river. Slowly slanting
the ground makes a casual descent down to the riverside, through a gradually
thickening foliage. Almost beckoning for a swim, the clear river lies calmly
ahead, stirred only by a gentle flow among the bracken dipping their roots in
the shallow water. To the west a narrow trail can be seen winding its way
through the grass up a cliff.
name: Sloping Meadow

desc:
A small gurgling stream is heading north along the border of an old forest. The
stream is rich in fresh water weeds, and there are several boulders on the
bottom which look promising should you decide to dip cork, string and hook with
the prospect of catching tonight's dinner. Not far off to the north, the stream
joins the Glanduin river, and together they journey for many miles west to the
sea. The open landscape to the west of the stream is coloured mainly in green,
and it continues as far as you can see.
name: Gentle Stream

desc:
Bushy pines with gnarled branches and sturdy stems stand closer than arm's
reach. Though the forest floor is mainly flat and easily travelled, there are
places where thorny bushes and tall grass form nasty barriers between the trees
and crossing puts heavy strain on both gear and limbs. From the north can be
heard the incessant sound of purling water.
name: Tangled Conifers

desc:
A forest of weary and intertwined pine trees forms dense thickets in a maze-
like fashion. Between the pine hedgerows green grass grows mixed with old spruce
needles from last year's fall. The trees are thinning to the north where they
are cut off by a river, and near the river grass and bushes take up more and
more space.
name: Tangled Conifers

desc:
Previously green with beautiful pine trees the forest has now turned dull and
grey. Most of the trees are dead and the few trees that remain alive struggle to
keep what's left of their green needles. The dead forest floor is covered in
brown needles that crunch and bounce with every step. North the vegetation
slowly comes to life, as dead trees give way to bracken and green grass that
grows effortlessly in the moist environment along the Glanduin riverbanks.
name: Withering Pines

desc:
South of the Glanduin river is a softwood forest. A handful of different tree
sorts can be seen, most of them have curly and crude branches, and are not very
tall. The forest floor of spruce needles is soft and there is little underbrush
to slacken the pace. Towards the riverbank to the north the trees are slowly
dispersed as millet grass and wild flowers claim ground.
name: Withering Pines

desc:
Old yew trees and twisted scotch pines grow amongst each other on the southern
bank of the Glanduin River. The bank slopes gently down to the riverside, where
the trees gradually disappear in favour of bracken and grass. The river, not far
away beyond the trees, can be heard as a clear purling sound of bubbles and
foam.
name: Withering Pines

desc:
In an area densly vegetated with pine trees and bracken, travelling has to be
done by foot. Even though the brackens are soft, clearing a way through the
undergrowth soon turns arms and body weary. The south is cast is shadows by an
ancient forest of tall trees with wide crowns blocking out most light.
name: Tangled Conifers

desc:
The untamed forest consist mainly of tall trees with bushes and shrubs tangled
about their stems. The ground present little elevation but nevertheless the
vegetation offers many difficulties for a journey through the forest. Birds and
rodents hide among the bushes in relative safety from predators. The vegetation
to the west prevents any further travel in that direction.
name: Ancient Forest

desc:
Bracken and heather covers the ground in a ancient forest of mixed trees. The
fertile earth is soft and fairly level. Bracken are easily pushed aside, but
sometimes the undergrowth changes into thick entangled bushes that cannot be
passed without much cutting and cursing. Trees and brushwood, both green and
bright, are in appearance so varied that it's hard to imagine any forestation
ever to have taken place; an observation also supported by the thick roof of
leaves formed by the tree-crowns above.
name: Ancient Forest

desc:
Swaying with majestic grace in the wind, the slender trees of the old forest
rustle a roof of dark-green leaves some twenty feet above. Between the trees
grows a dense underwood that makes a mounted attempt to travel the forest
impossible. A shallow stream trickles north along the forest floor, sometimes
disappearing under dense bushes. Although the vegetation is very dense you catch
a glimpse of a stone-structure to the west.
name: Ancient Forest

desc:
The forest of tall trees standing only a few feet apart is not easily travelled
as the brushwood of bushes and shrubs grows dense with thorns and constricting
vines that entangle legs and arms. The ground, covered in brown leaves, is dry
and provides a good hiding space for the many animals that live in the forest.
Looking up the view is blocked by a landscape of dark green leaves that makes it
impossible to determine whether it's night or day.
name: Ancient Forest

desc:
Consisting mainly of tall deciduous trees and thickets of low bushes the green
woodland is somewhat easily travelled. The soft forest floor is cast in brown
and orange by withered leaves and bone-dry branches that crack on impact. The
dark shadow from the dense tree-crowns above, plays mysteriously on the ground
that is cold and never lit by the sun. To the south the forest is gradually
thickening with dark bushes, and visibility in that direction is poor.
name: Ancient Forest

desc:
The old hardwood forest rustle its dry branches in the wind shaking of dust and
cobwebs. The forest floor, beneath the slender stems that surround you on all
sides, is dry, and with each step you hear the crunching sounds of old leaves
and twigs snapping. Above hovers a dense foliage of dark-green leaves which
cloakes the underwood in dark cold shadow. A small stream takes its begining
here, gentle clucks and purls sends it northward at a slow pace.
name: Ancient Forest

desc:
A dense undergrowth among the tall trees in the forest hinders travel through
this region. Bushes and shrubs, with thorny and torpid twigs, tangle gear and
legs as were they insects caught in a spiders web. A line of trees with thick
thornbushes at their feet grow on an escarpment to the south, passing that way
is impossible. Above the tree tops form a dense layer of leaves blocking out
light.
name: Ancient Forest

desc:
No wind moves through the quiet forest. A low wood-embosomed clearing enjoys the
indisturbed tranquillity in the midst of a ancient forest. The clearing, created
by a felling long time ago, is more than fifty feet across and covered with soft
moss and heather. The otherwise anxious birds elsewhere in these woods seem to
have settled with your presence in this clearing. A log of a fallen tree, now
almost withered away, rests heavily on a dirt surface in the middle of the
clearing where grass and heather are less dominant. In an odd way the trees
surrounding the clearing bend inwards making the clearing very dark.
name: Dark Opening in the Ancient Forest

desc:
Among tall trees of mixed sort exists a multifarious plantlife. Unhindered by
the trees, that steal most of the light in the forest, the lower forest has
developed into a fertile habitat supporting plants not normally found growing
together. Groundsel, Corydalis and Goldenrod seems to grow especially well in
these conditions. It's hard to find a way around the plantbeds that often blocks
the way between the trees. East the plants are gradually dispersed as the forest
opens into a clearing.
name: Ancient Forest

desc:
Thick bushes with dark-green leaves makes the area very difficult to travel.
Branches and vines twists together in a messy bungle quite impossible to open
up. Nasty thorns makes it even worse, and there is no option but to find a way
around the worst of the thickets. The darkness created in the shadow of the
tree-crowns above makes navigation the forest even harder. North the bushes thin
out while more and more trees rises. To the west both trees and bushes diminish
as the forest seems to take an end there.
name: Dense Brushwood

desc:
Wild thorny bushes tangled into dense thickets hinders any traveller wishing to
make way through the vegetation. In the thickets can be seen hawthorn and holly
bushes with red berries. The clucking sound of trickling water reaches you from
the west, a stream must be near. To the east the brushwood continues and also
tall trees are begining to show.
name: Brushwood

desc:
A dense undergrowth among mixed tree sorts slows movement through this region.
The vegetation is varied and rich, although bracken has taken up most space. On
the trees grow different types lichen, some green and curly other grey-brown
with round bodily knobs; the seem to thrive in the dark shadows of a roof of
dark-green leaves above. From the northeast falls a brighter light suggesting
clearing.
name: Ancient Forest

desc:
The trees stand only a few feet apart in the ancient forest, and interspersed
between the tall stems grows a brushwood thick and thorny. Forcing a way through
the underbrush has mostly bruises and scratches as result, and often a different
route must be found. A dense and intertwined roof of dark-green leaves
effectively cuts off most light from the forest. To the north an upwards slope
with dense vegetation at the top prohibits any travel.
name: Ancient Forest

desc:
Crude earthwalls, slanting and uneven, forms this dark earthen burrow beneath an
ancient forest. Roots and boulders left in the walls underlines the sloopy work
that lead to the construction of the burrow. The muddy floor is a mix of dirt,
filthy rags and bones crushed into a state where their origin can no longer be
determined. The smell is nauseating to say the least, and it is clear that this
is the insanitary home of very filthy creatures. The trail of bones and rags
points south, further into the ground.
name: Filthy Burrow

desc:
Slanting earthen walls lean inward forming a dome-shaped burrow about twenty
feet wide. Twisting roots and large rocks protruding from the surface of the
walls hinder movement, but judging by the size of the footprints left in the wet
mud on the ground, a mere root would not be a problem for one of the
inhabitants. Cracked bones and torn fabric litter the floor, and the remains of
weapons and armour, enough to equip a small army, have been dropped to rust in a
corner. A fireplace in the centre of the burrow, large enough to roast a cow or
a bear, has a convenient outlet in the roof.
name: Filthy Burrow

desc:
A scattered population of pine trees stand dormantly around you shaking their
branches in the howling wind. The forest floor is covered in green grass. To the
east the forest silently claims more space, and the visibility in that direction
is low compared the the west where the forest gives way to open plains. In the
southwest a mountain ridge rises above the forest border.
name: Forest Border

desc:
Located at the foot of an inaccesible mountain ridge is a richly vegetated area.
Man-sized bushes and bracken grow in green clusters in the soft soil. The
purling sound of a shallow stream coming from the south is echoed against the
tall cliff to the west. East the vegetation becomes more compact as the bracken
is forced back by thornbushes and trees that mark the begining of a forest. A
small trail around the mountain ridge winds through the vegetation while
following closely the contours of the stream.
name: Trail through Bushes and Bracken

desc:
Beneath the tall peaks of a mountain ridge the ground slopes upward until it
meets a steep mountain side. On the sloping hillside vegetation is scarce,
partly due to the permanent shadow of the mountains. The ground is rocky, yet
stable enough to allow mounted travellers passage over the slanting hillside
beneath the mountains. North the terrain flattens as the endless plains of
Dunland begin.
name: Hillside

desc:
In a corner, between the mountains to the south and an old forest to the east,
lies a peaceful meadow vegetated by grass, small bushes and dense banks of
weeds. Tiny buttercups with yellow eyes shine from the western part of the
meadow, where the ground becomes more rocky and hilly. To the east, under the
shadow of a tall old forest, delicious mushrooms can be found growing wild.
name: Green Meadow

desc:
Out of the waist-high grass rise the ruined stone walls of a decayed manor. The
remaining walls, now partially overgrown with lichen and grass, are thick and
strong, capable of withstanding the rough weather in the open country. The
ground between the ruins is soft and the plantlife along the walls is rich and
varied. Among lush green bracken, that covers the walls almost three feet from
the ground, grows a dense layer of goutweed and dandelions; could the bracken be
hiding something else? To the east lies the border of an ancient forest of tall
slender trees.
name: Ruins by a Stream

desc:
In what was previously a prison cell in the basement of a manor, the ceiling has
collapsed bringing down with it much earth and timber from the structure that
once housed many men above. The fate of the prisoners trapped beneath the wreck
is obvious, and a feeling of anger over the prolonged imprisonment or early
death fills the air. A large gaping hole in the ceiling is partially covered by
the vegetation that grows above the hole.
name: Ancient Prison Cell

desc:
A quiet stream purls gently as it flows north on the western side of an old
forest. Lush green grass grows on the banks on either side of the stream, and
further to the east bordering up the to the forest begins a dense growth of
hogweed and stinging nettles. The grey ruins of a manor can be seen among the
grass further south.
name: Gentle Stream

desc:
A mixture of different pine trees, mingled in hedgerows with thornbushes and
tall grass, surrounds you on all sides. The forest floor is covered with spruce
needles and old withering twigs and branches that creak beneath your feet. Under
brushwood and trees grows a multitude of flowers, herb and fungi. A small stream
coming from the south darts through the forest heading west.
name: Tangled Conifers

desc:
Trees of two origins meet here. Low, but sturdy, conifers from the north are
suddenly overtaken by a tall deciduous forest from the south. The trees have
mixed over the years and recently a dense brushwood has joined to make any
passage through the forest both difficult and tiresome. A sad view of dying
trees meets you to the east.
name: Forest Transition

desc:
The once thriving pine forest is now dead and withering away. The trees were
once green and healthy, but some disease must have caused the early quietus of
the forest. The trees that still bear needles are scarce and ready to submit to
their fate at any time, the ones already dead now host lichen and fungi. To the
south the dead pine trees stop as a hardwood forest of taller trees begins.
name: Withering Pines

desc:
Old and withered are the trees in the coniferous forest located south of the
Glanduin river. A thick bouncing layer of dry spruce needles covers the forest
floor. Some of the pine trees are long since dead, only lichen and fungi are
left to find a last use of the wounded branches. South the flora changes to
taller trees and a dense undergrowth, and from the north is heard the faint
sound of the river flowing slowly through the landscape.
name: Withering Pines

desc:
Despite an overwhelming presence of lush green grass, the flora in the plains of
Dunland is varied and breathtakingly beautiful. Dandelions, violets and poppy
bloom with a stunning palette of colours and scents in the fecund soil, that
provides a nutritious environment for the healthy vegetation. A myriad of
insects and creepie-crawlers live among the roots and in the soil, only
disturbed by the gentle gurgle of a small stream to the east.
name: Dunland Plains

desc:
The plains of Dunland hosts a wide range of different plants among which the
various types of green grass are the most dominant. But among the green grass
one might stumble onto a meadow like this where, for a while, the grass plays a
lesser role in the flora and wild flowers stand pretty and tall among a green
covering of weeds. To the north a small trail is barely visible among the grass.
name: Dunland Plains

desc:
The windswept plains of Dunland fill the horizon with a scenic view of green
grass thriving in the fertile soil. Besides a few dirt- and rubble- filled holes
the ground is even; low bushes and shubbery combat the almost waist-heigh grass
for space, and even wild flowers make a lonely mark here and there. A thin dark
line a few miles to the north denotes the presence of some kind of trail.
name: Dunland Plains

desc:
The wide open plains of Dunland sweep across the horizon creating a scenic yet
monotone view. Green grass flourish in the moist and fertile soil, and between
turfs and grassy banks is found a variety of different wild flowers; some bear
red crowns of bitter-smelling leaves. In the distance the waveing sway of the
green grass blends into the horizon in a orange-yellow haze.
name: Dunland Plains

desc:
From this position on the mostly flat plain you see the Misty Mountains rising
from the flats, creating a jagged landscape on the southern horizon. A high peak
reaches for the sky in the south-east, and further east the hills grow even
higher. Some, at least in comparison flat, grass-covered hills bulge in the
west. The yellow but mostly green plains lie to the north.
name: Dunland Plains

desc:
The plain slopes discreetly upwards in the west until the steep slope of a hill
blocks the way. In the south-west you can't but notice a spur from the Misty
Mountains reaching out in the plains. Some quite large boulders have rolled down
from the hill in the west, now offering dark shelter for snakes and other
reptiles.
name: Sloping Plain

desc:
From halfway up the slope to a hill in the south you can see the contours of
quite a few hills bulging in the west. In the east you see the rounded and
grassy hilltops being overtaken by the rough tops of the Misty Mountains. Drying
grass dominates the immediate surroundings but as if they wanted to alleviate
the dullness there some soft flowers growing by your feet.
name: Sloping Plain

desc:
Grass-covered plains rise into low hills and slopes at the foot of a western
spur in the Misty Mountains. The surface of the wasteland ground is hard and
doesn't breed life to much else but the endless sea of green grass and a few
clusters of wild flowers. Yet patches of thin bushes have settled in the shelter
of a low depression to the southeast, where the hills begin their climb towards
the peaks of the mountains.
name: Rising Plains

desc:
A trail of boulders leads the way through a steep climb in the mountains. A
recent rock-slide sent boulders tumbling down the rugged mountain side and now
the climb, that leads between jagged peaks, has become dangerous and not to be
challenged by unskilled climbers. The slope bends upwards to the south, and to
the west the terrain becomes less rocky as it gradually turns into hills.
name: Mountain Climb

desc:
The craggy mountains provide only meager living conditions for the unvaried
flora consisting mainly of scruffy moss and dry flowers. Here an escarpment
connects the plains below the mountain range to a small plateau beneath the
rocky peaks to the southeast. Slim crevices braid together a path of strong
handholds suitable for traversing the southern part of the slope. North the rock
takes a sudden dive, creating a vertical drop of several fathoms to the plains
below.
name: Steep Mountain Slope

desc:
A grey mountain range to the south forms a high wall overlooking the foothills
which unfold into a bumpy terrain of grass and bushes further on a few miles
into the north. The ground is cold, confined to rest in shadow under the misty
peaks of the mountains. In a quiet hollow between two neighbouring hills grows a
small thicket of green bushes.
name: Rising Plains

desc:
Mile after mile the sea of grass and weeds continues in all directions. Besides
the always common variants of grass, there are also thick beds of goutweed and
stinging needles. The terrain is flat and unchallenging travel, but a mile or
two to the south the land turns into foothills before a mountain range. Far to
the east lies a dark and secret forest.
name: Dunland Plains

desc:
An endless view of thick-nestled grass fills the horizon in every direction.
Mixed with occasional wild flowers and wind-beaten bushes, the grassy plains
form a gracious contour to the mountain peaks far in the east. The terrain is
mostly flat, besides a series of low trenches that surround what appears to be a
formerly farmed area. Barren since many years, the field has merged with the
wasteland plains.
name: Dunland Plains

desc:
The barren plains compose an endless view of grass bending and swaying in the
wind. Only occasional bumps of soil and ruffled grass rise up, in a futile
rejection to submit to the endless beating from the the rough conditions in
weather found in the Dunland Plains. Besides a few low bushes seeking refuge in
scarce depressions, all other vegetation is outnumbered by the always dominant
thickets of stifled grass.
name: Dunland Plains

desc:
Little shelter can be found in the open plains of Dunland. Here the weather sets
the agenda; often, with fierce determination and force, the wind sweeps across
the open land, gnawing its chilly teeth through any barrier in its way. The
soil, being partly rubble and fist-sized stones, is little comfort to the
scattered and unvaried vegetation of grass and low bushes.
name: Dunland Plains

desc:
Beneath the widespread fingers of the Misty Mountains, that take shape far into
the east, lies a small strip of rough and weatherbeaten turf. Grass and bushes
struggle in the unforgiving conditions where the only shelter can be found to
the south where a dormant mountain range with spiked towers of grey rock
glitters through a thin cover of mist. The ground slopes steadily upward to the
south, and gradually grass and bushes give way to stones and dull green moss.
name: Rising Plains

desc:
A thin mountain ridge reaches out from a mountain range going east-west south of
here. Moving across the ridge is a dangerous climb with many loose rocks on the
way. Vegetation here is sparse, limited to dry grass and tiny bushes tugging
their roots into the soil and rubble found infrequently throughout the many
cracks and scars in the rock. The ridge provides an excellent view over the
plains of Dunland to the north and over the treetops of a mixed forest to the
east.
name: High Mountain Ridge

desc:
The mountain terrain is challenging even for a skilled climber. Yet a rough
ledge, beneath the the mountain peaks, provides as much comfort as is needed to
endure the hardship of travelling through the misty cliffs. Beneath the mountain
wall lie gaping gorges with sharp splintered rock-teeth waiting only to bite.
Descending slightly, yet determined, a lower ridge bends away from the mountain
to the north; it might be possible to leave the dangerous mountains that way.
name: Mountain Cliff

desc:
Overlooking the Dunland Plains is an exposed cliff-side under the great peaks of
the Misty Mountains. The cliff is formed of the rough stone, that extends
further west before it begins its steady decent into the plains. In the high
mountains vegetation is scarce, limited to only a few patches of moss and the
occasional wild flower. A thin mist swirls over the peaks to the east, limiting
the view of further travel into the mountains.
name: Mountain Cliff

desc:
Steep sloping hills strive to overlook each other as one after another they
spread out beneath the overwhelming mountain peaks of the Misty Mountains to the
east. Sturdy bushes and thick grass, struggle in the unforgiving wind that
sweeps through the foothills. Boulders and mountain siplings pierce through the
soil at many a hilltop and slope, exposed to the weather by the retreating
vegetation.
name: Rising Hills

desc:
This hill forms the westernmost outpost of the great Misty Mountains. The
grasses grow sparse on the slopes down to the plain below but dwindle even more
in the east leaving only a few bushes to protect the earth from stones and
boulders tearing and ripping on their way toward the plain below.
name: Hilly Range

desc:
The rocky ground inhibits substantial vegetation growth, though a few small
bushes and a sapling or two struggle to take root. Loose rocks and a few leaves
lie dormant upon the ground, covering an ancient trail which traverses the
terrain from north to south, providing passage from the valley to the Misty
Mountains.
name: Rocky Trail

desc:
These rough hills descend to the south and west while to the north and east they
lead towards the mountains. Directly east there is a small chamber beneath a
rough stone arch that crosses from one mountain peak to another. The arch,
clearly visible from here, is a magnificent work of nature. It is more than one
hundred feet long and over three feet wide and often coated by a thin layer of
ice. To the east, beneath the arch, you can see the skeletal remains of those
who lost their footing while crossing the bridge.
name: Rough Hills

desc:
Against the side of the mountain, to the east, the plains suffer from the lack
of water and harsh winds. Once a long time ago a few hardy plants took root, but
now all have withered and died leaving their crusty remains. There are no signs
of animals on the ground or in the air for this place is truly barren.
name: Withered Plains

desc:
Bounded by the Misty Mountains to the south and an ancient forest to the north
little vegetation has managed to grow here. The soil consists mostly of small
chunks of granite mixed with dirt and a few brittle leaves from the nearby
forest. While a few blades of grass have managed to take root, as in previous
attempts, they are likely to soon wither and die. To the north the forest begins
to grow in earnest.
name: Forest Border

desc:
To the north an ancient forest rises above you contrasting starkly with the
mountains to the south. While the forest is densely packed with ancient trees
and shallow shrubs, this region is only lightly foliaged. The ground, mostly a
thin layer of soil upon a rocky substrate, is hard and unyielding to the roots
of the few small shrubs that manage to grow here.
name: Forest Border

desc:
This lightly forested area forms a transition between the Misty Mountains to the
north and an ancient forest to the south. Grass can be seen growing at the base
of the mountains and slowly giving way to some small ferns and coarse bushes.
Here and there a small tree can be seen sprouting.
name: Forest Border

desc:
Only light vegetation and small trees have managed to survive the rocky
conditions of this area, boxed in by the Misty Mountains to the south and a vast
ancient forest to the north. While little can be seen to the south where the
mountains rise before you, sounds and cries of wild animals can be heard from
the north.
name: Forest Border

desc:
Years of erosion as a small underground mountain stream flowed through the
granite rock have caused the ceiling to collapse, forming this overhang some
fifteen feet above the stream. While the sides are wet, making climbing somewhat
difficult, the water below ensures a soft landing if you should fall. To the
west, a difficult trip across the mountain leads towards a barren plain.
name: Rocky Overhang

desc:
High above plains to the west and south you can see that much of the land has
become barren. Long ago these were fruitful farmlands, but years of fighting
followed by prolonged drought and isolation have left this land barren. To the
north and east the mountains continue, blocking much of your vision. You can
continue west and south onto the plains or, with great effort, east into the
mountains.
name: West Side of the Misty Mountains

desc:
Here, these plains form a stark contrast as the flat land ceases to the north
and east where the Misty Mountains rise from the earth. While the plains are
barren and consist mostly of brittle soil, little more than sand, the mountains
are composed of a near vertical wall of rugged granite, which nature
inadvertently provided as protection from all but the most invasive of
intruders.
name: Withered Plains

desc:
Sparse barren plains surround you in all directions. To the south the plains,
while still dry and barren, begin to show some signs of life. To the east, even
from this distance, the mountains are clearly visible with the harsh features of
the rough granite sides making their presence known.
name: Withered Plains

desc:
Often a sharp wind blows across the land producing a shrill whistle as it comes
off the mountains to the north and east. A long time ago, a few hardy plants
managed to survive these harsh conditions but a prolonged drought brought an end
to their life. With their removal the wind quickly laid waste to the top soil
making the return of plant life unlikely.
name: Withered Plains

desc:
These hills, a precursor to the Misty Mountains, contain an abundance of ragged
boulders. While some of the rocks are little more than stones, many of them are
larger than the largest troll. The gaps between the boulders make travel slow
and difficult. To the north and east the Misty Mountains can be found, while to
the west and south a vast barren plain stretches across the landscape.
name: Rough Hills

desc:
These plains are barren, void of both plant and animal life. Often a cloud of
dust hangs overhead greatly reducing visibility. A soft shrill can be heard from
time to time as the wind races through the gaps in the mountains to the north
and east. Sheltered by the mountains, little water reaches this area leaving the
soil brittle, hard and dry.
name: Withered Plains

desc:
These plains have been barren for many years. After the few plants that grew in
this region lost life, the harsh wind removed the fertile top soil. With little
water and poor soil, plants have been unable to regain a foothold in this
region, leaving these plains barren. Little in the way of landmarks
distinguishes this area from those nearby, and only the Misty Mountains to the
east provide a meaningful landmark.
name: Withered Plains

desc:
Much of northern Dunland lies under the Misty Mountains, their ominous presence
dominating the eastern skyline. Here is no different - they are under 10 miles
away from these plains. Most of the rest of the horizon is flat however, miles
upon miles of the same landscape. Here the winds have stripped the flat topsoil
of all its nutrients, leaving it barren and rocky. No plants grow here in the
dusty earth.
name: Arid Flatlands

desc:
Withered and dusty like most of the surrounding plains, these windswept lands
are near completely barren of life. The occasional hardy shrub manages to poke
through the rocky earth, but little more does so. To the north and northeast a
hilly spur of the Misty Mountains thrusts outwards into the plains, its steep
side facing you.
name: Withered Plain

desc:
Across the plains little can be seen. The air is choked full of soil and the
ground is littered by the long dead remains of plants. To the north the ground
begins to slope upwards as a hill forms a buffer between these barren plains and
more fruitful places. To the east the Misty Mountains begin to take shape some
six miles away.
name: Withered Plains

desc:
The steep hill rises sharply to the north and to the south it quickly falls as
it merges into a vast barren plain. Often the region to the south is covered by
dust as the wind lifts the barren soil into the air where it remains for days.
While the taller hills to the north often block the airborne soil, here much of
it collects both in the air and upon the ground making the ground silky and
slick. To the east some six miles away, even when conditions are harsh, the
rising slopes of the Misty Mountains are visible.
name: Rising Hills

desc:
This hill is the last in a range running out from the Misties. Although nowhere
near as high as the main mountain range, it is still a fair high climb to its
height. Its southern flank is the steepest, and would require climbing to get
down to the plains. North and east the hills continue, while to the south and
west the plains continue endlessly.
name: Protruding Hill

desc:
Green grass waves slowly as the wind caresses the broken ground around you. Long
grass blades extend towards the sky and some of them seem to make a serious
attempt of actually reaching it. Some hilly ridges confine the other bank of the
slowly flowing river in the north.
name: Dunland Plains

desc:
A very large, mossy stone is firmly grounded in the earth surrounded with miles
of grass in all directions. Except for the stone, there are only some tiny
irregularities in the horizon to break the monotony of green grass with dried
yellow blades here and there.
name: Dunland Plains

desc:
A vast open field of mossy banks and turfs dominates every inch in range of
sight. Buttercup mixed with crowfoot in tight clusters makes a picturesque view
as they fade from bright yellow into lush green where the ever imposing beds of
grass prevail over the minuscule occurrence of low green-stemmed bushes. The
flat land is loose and forgiving to the feet or hooves that might travel through
the fields of Dunland.
name: Dunland Plains

desc:
Wild flowers and waist-high grass intermingle on the open plains. There is
little variation in the green surroundings which seem to go on forever if not
for the grey shadow of several ruins to the northeast where the plains are
abruptly halted by a river. To the north and the east is a trail that appear to
be passing through the ruins.
name: Dunland Plains

desc:
Travelling the green fields of Dunland would soon become a tiring experience, if
not the for varied flora and buzzing insectlife. Everywhere there is a new plant
or rock to admire and explore, and butterflies are flapping their colourful
wings in the breeze is a constant distraction from the road ahead. Suddenly a
shadow becomes apparent to the east, a dense forest rises its tall crowns high
in the air a few miles away. Just before the forest stand the walls of a ruined
manor.
name: Dunland Plains

desc:
The country rises with gentle hills in a region mainly vegetated by grass and
weeds. Some areas have been scorched by the sun to form little brown islands
among the green grass; there the ground is dry and the withered grass crumbles
beneath your feet. A few miles to the south stands a tall mountain ridge with
majestic snow-capped peaks. To the east lies a hardwood forest with tall green
crowns casting a deep shadow among the stems.
name: Grassy Foothills

desc:
A strip of stiff-grassed fairway is wedged between the bulging foothills that
spread out in front of the steep slopes of a mountain range to the south. The
green grass flourishes in the fecund soil, beneath the protective cliffs of the
mountain. The elevation progresses in the hills nigh the mountains until in the
end it begins climbing steadily upwards. East the hills fade out before the dark
shadow of a forest.
name: Grassy Foothills

desc:
Beneath a short overhang a comfortable plateau spans across two cliffs. The
southern cliff quickly makes a climbable descent further into the mountain
range, but to the north the cliff rises towards the most notable peak in sight.
The suddenly level cliff is well protected between the cliffs, yet accessible
from all sides, except to the north. Under a low scoop in the northern cliff is
a small pile of fist-sized rocks.
name: Mountain Plateau

desc:
You are upon a narrow icy bridge, near the mountain's boundary with the plains,
which extends across an enormous chasm. The bridge is nearly three feet wide and
some thirty feet above the ground. While the ground below is composed of soft
sand, a few sharp rocks make any sort of decent chancy at best.
name: Mountain Bridge

desc:
Midway up the side the mountain you can see across the naked plains to the west
and south. To the east a steep, near vertical, incline makes it unlikely that
you will be able to proceed in that direction. However, a gap to the north does
allow passage.
name: Mountain Side

desc:
Below the arches, the colourful bands of rock which form the mountain pass above
are clearly visible. There are at least a dozen bands of rock, each a unique
shade ranging from deep brown to rusty orange. While some sunlight can creep in
during early morning, this place for the most part is left in the shadows during
the day. The ground is soft and silky, composed of very fine grains of dust that
the wind and rain slowly chiselled from the rock which forms the basis of the
giant arches.
name: Beneath the Mountain Pass

desc:
The remnants of plants that have lost their battle with this dry land lay here
crumbling into nothing. A few weeds, that seem to need less moisture, continue
to struggle between the occasional rains. The rain, which rarely falls, is never
enough to satisfy all. North and east, the Misty Mountains loom ominously
overhead.
name: Withered Plains

desc:
Towering above the plains the Misty Mountains cast enormous shadows onto the
barren ground below. Wind cascades down the slopes, blowing dust into the eyes
of travellers. The mountains rise sheer to the north, prohibiting access in that
direction. The base of the mountains continues eastward, while dry barren plains
stretch to the west and south.
name: Base of the Misty Mountains

desc:
Rising up from the plains is the base of the Misty Mountains yawning
precariously overhead. Large broken boulders lie strewn about the landscape,
warning the traveller to be wary of potential landslides. A chill dry wind blows
down the mountainsides, causing dust to swirl about in small but rapid
whirlwinds. The steep rise in the mountains to the north prevent any passage in
that direction. To the east the Misty Mountains begin in earnest. and south dry
plains begin to take hold.
name: Base of the Misty Mountains

desc:
The Misty Mountains climb high, on reaching their peak somewhere north of here.
Their height provides an excellent view of the countryside south and west, which
is mostly dry flatlands. Southward the mountains begin to give way to a wooded
grove of trees. There appears to be no way to travel further north. East, the
mountains continue ever onward.
name: Misty Mountains

desc:
Tall grey snow-capped mountains prevent any further journey to the east and
north. The Misty Mountains continue to the south and west, lessening as they
fall to the plains. Fallen broken boulders litter the ground, an indication of
the threat that rockslides occur frequently here.
name: Impassable Mountain

desc:
These drying and decayed plains bear witness to the difficult conditions of this
area. The severe and unforgiving winds that blow down from the Misty Mountains
have stripped away the fertile soil, leaving these plains barren. Only a few
sparse bushes provide distinguishing landmarks in the nearby vicinity. Off in
the distance, to the northeast, the Misty Mountains rise up with their
snow-capped peaks.
name: Withered Plains

desc:
Strewn about the landscape, large rocks and boulders begin to form the base of
the Misty Mountains. The ground here is rocky and very little grows except
perhaps the occasional shrub. To the west, barren plains begin to take hold of
the landscape, while a wooded grove starts up to the south.
name: Base of the Misty Mountains

desc:
Death, decay and decadence are this cave's decor. Stains of blood mixed with
orkish draught create ugly patterns on the floor and walls. A musty odour hangs
in the air like a heavy fog. Rotten bones of an old kill lie under a bench,
badly decomposing.
name: An Orkish Lair

desc:
The musty smell of rotten meat lingers in the air. Two piles of torn human
clothes have been arranged to soften the floor for sleeping. The clothes seem to
harbour several parasitic organisms which feed on the ripped fibres. No light or
fresh air enters here, and one can assume these clothes may have belonged to the
same victims as the bones in the nearby lair.
name: Den of Orkish Slumber

desc:
The Misty Mountains continue their climb north, reaching their summit somewhere
far to the north of here. From this vantage point, one is afforded an excellent
view of the surrounding lands south and west of this location. To the southwest
the mountains begin to give way to a wooded grove of trees.
name: Misty Mountains

desc:
Growing in the shade of the Misty Mountains plains take hold, although somewhat
sparse and dry. They provide little shelter however, from the strong winds that
blow down from high up in the snow-capped mountains. Rocky rubble, from the
nearby mountain base, has fallen here, littering the landscape with countless
boulders. To the south trees begin to take hold, and thicken into a grove.
name: Dry Plains

desc:
The plants here grow in the shade of the great Misty Mountains looming overhead
to the north. Their shelter breaks down many a strong wind and protects from
what can be a burning sun. Rocky rubble, from the nearby mountain's base, has
been tracked here.
name: Dry Plains

desc:
A group of young pines try to take seed in this place. Not much taller than ten
feet they provide little shade nor shelter from occasional winds. Whether they
will survive to tower above is doubtful given the lack of any such woods in the
nearby lands. The arid nature of the land has perhaps already sealed their
