fates. The plains continue in all directions, only stopped eastward by the
towering mountains.
name: Dry Plains

desc:
Not as dry here as to the north, this land is bit more hospitable. A cluster of
prickly weeds catches dirt and the dried up leaves of some nearby plants that
have died from lack of nourishment. Some rocks too heavy to lift are embedded
here in the dirt.
name: Dry Plains

desc:
The plain is rather dry and desolate. Little vegetation can be seen, save for an
occasional patch of grass here and there. Rocks and stones, however, can be
found in a multitude. To the west and south, you see a trail bending around the
plains.
name: Dry Plains

desc:
What vegetation lies here is sadly withering. Lack of moisture in the air and
the soil condemns these few small plants and shrubs to a short life, if one at
all. Many of the smaller growths have already succumbed. To the east more plant
life struggles to survive. Further east, the Misty Mountains provide an ominous
backdrop to these plains.
name: Withered Plains

desc:
Mountain peaks loom high above the base of these Misty Mountains blocking any
view one might have to the east. Glancing eastward, one can see the entrance to
a mine under an overhang in the rock. To the west, a wooded grove of trees takes
hold and flourishes. Looking southward, a cliff face falls away before your
feet. At its base a small stream tumbles along, on its journey to the sea.
name: Base of the Misty Mountains

desc:
Small pine trees line the edges of a swift flowing brook. The water's edge,
lined with algae and water lilies, teems with insect life. To the east and west
the stream continues. The trees begin to fade to the south, where rolling plains
pick up and dominate the landscape.
name: Babbling Brook

desc:
Lofty conifer trees rise high up high into the air, while a thick web of foliage
blankets the ground, leaving little to no trace of visitors. The base of the
Misty Mountains can just be seen peaking out from the trees north and east of
this grove. The faint sound of rushing water can be heard to the south and the
thinning grove continues west.
name: Thinning Woodland Grove

desc:
Tall pine trees stretch overhead and a thick mesh of foliage carpets the ground.
The conifers densely begin to crowd together to the south. North and west, the
trees abruptly stop and give way to a vast expanse of open plains. The grove
continues east, just before the mountains.
name: Thinning Woodland Grove

desc:
Wide, open plains immediately surround and encompass you. A little distance to
the north, the plains begin to thin out. Further north, the Misty Mountains rise
high up into the sky. To the east and south, a woodland grove springs up and
continues south. The fertile plains continue westward.
name: Open Plains

desc:
The grass grows low and is cropped short by strong winds, which persistently
blow down from somewhere high up in the Misty Mountains. Trees begin to spring
up south and east of here, forming a small woodland to the southeast. A trail,
making a slow bend around these plains, lies to the south and west.
name: Open Plains

desc:
Bending back and forth across the open plains, the trail continues west and
south from here. To the northeast, the plains dry out as they approach the base
of the Misty Mountains. Southwest, however, the plains are green and lush with
life.
name: Bend in the Trail

desc:
Following the bend, this trail makes its way east and south alongside a great
distance of plains, both to the north and to the south. However, the plains to
the north appear more dry and arid. A steady wind sweeps across the ground,
cracking and hardening the exposed soil.
name: Dunlending Trail

desc:
A meadowed plain of short lush green grass lies here and stretches north, whilst
the northern horizon is dominated by the Misty Mountains. The grass grows higher
to the west, while a well worn trail lies north and east of here, curving along
the outside of the plains.
name: Boundless Meadow

desc:
The running of water cascading through rocks and tree roots can be heard, and a
small brook can be seen flowing through this grove of towering pine trees. The
trees appear to thin to the east and north, while densely continuing west and
south.
name: Woodland Grove beside a Brook

desc:
Tall close-knit pine trees crowd in from all directions, impeding but not
altogether halting travel through this grove. A lush mat of green foliage
carpets the ground, hiding the footprints of past travellers. The trees appear
to thin out toward the north and west, while densely continuing south and east.
name: Woodland Grove

desc:
Rocks and pebbles, deeply embedded into the roadway, give evidence of heavy use.
The trail continues south, bending around a grove of towering pines, and west
across a wide expanse of open plains. Far in the distance to the southeast faint
wisps of smoke can be seen rising into the sky.
name: Bend in the Trail

desc:
Constant winds drift down from high up in the Misty Mountains, sweeping across
these plains. The path that lies before you is at no risk of becoming obscured
as signs of recent use have packed down the soil into an easily recognisable
trail. Far off in the east, faint wisps of smoke can be seen rising into the
sky.
name: Bend in the Trail

desc:
Waves of waist high grass ripple as the wind blows across these plains. A
endless sea of lush green plains stretch out in all other directions, the grass
growing taller to the south and thinning out a bit as one travels to the north.
name: Boundless Plains

desc:
An immense, open grassland broadens before you in all directions. A few sparsely
interspersed trees rise up from the carpet of grass, dotting the landscape. To
the north a small path meanders from east to west. East and south the vast green
plains continue.
name: Open Grasslands

desc:
This path shows signs of more age then use. Only a few brave blades of nearby
grass dare cross into the dirt marked for travel. A few stones, more the size of
pebbles, have been ground into the dirt. The trail continues east and west from
here. Plains lie on both sides of the path, the south being lush and green with
life, while dry and arid to the north.
name: Worn Path

desc:
The arid soil here barely manages to accommodate even the wild weeds, which
thicken to the south. Those that do grow siphon every bit of water out of the
soil and leave the surrounding ground dry and dusty. To the south and west, a
worn trail bends around the plains.
name: Dry Plains

desc:
The wind, when it blows, lifts the layer of fine dust-like topsoil that covers
this land. The dirt has completely infiltrated all of the few grassy weeds and
bushes growing in this dry domain, making everything a near uniform grey-green
colour. Rain is always welcome here, although it soaks into the dry land almost
as quickly as it falls to the ground. South of here, a faint trail cuts through
the plains, seeking a more hospitable landscape.
name: Dry Plains

desc:
The path continues to guide the way across wide-open plains. Looking down, one
can see the trail's recent use, marked by scores of footprints. To the north the
plains appear to dry out, leaving brown stunted grass. Southward, the grasslands
become thick and lush with green growth. The faint trail continues west and
east, where it begins to widen.
name: A Faint Trail

desc:
A vast sea of lush grassland stretches west and south from here. Several lone
trees rise up from the waves of green grass, dotting the landscape. To the north
and east a trail bends around these open plains. Far off to the northeast, the
Misty Mountains provide a backdrop to the plains.
name: Open Grasslands

desc:
A tall, luxuriant sea of waist high grass ripples as the wind gusts across this
prairie. Plains stretch out in every direction as far as the eye can see, the
grass growing higher to the south and shorter as one makes their way further
north.
name: Boundless Plains

desc:
A ceaseless expanse of plains stretches before you, a light wind rippling across
the grasslands and causing ripples in the undergrowth. The lush sea of green
plains continues in all directions as far as the eye can see, growing taller to
the southwest and cropped shorter to the northeast. A trail can be seen to the
north, bending south and heading east.
name: Boundless Plains

desc:
Paved firmly into the ground by thousands of footprints, this trail begins a
winding descent from north to south. As the path bends east, looking south, one
can see an endless see of lush plains. Wildlife can be seen scurrying through
the plains, their heads dipping in and out of the tall grasses.
name: Dunlending Trail

desc:
The path begins to widen here, as signs of recent use increase. The trail
continues west and south. Plains lie on both sides of the path, but appear green
and lush to the southwest, while dry and arid to the north. Far off to the
northeast, the Misty Mountains rise high into the sky.
name: Widening Trail

desc:
Sparse, barren plains unfold in all directions, becoming withered and decayed to
the north and east. The barren flatness continue west, while a small faint path
burrows its way through the dry-lands south of here. To the northeast, the Misty
Mountains rise up before you.
name: Dry Plains

desc:
A low cliff overhang hides a dark opening in the side of the mountain where the
ground has been artificially levelled. The rock surrounding the entrance is grey
and dull, aiding little support or shelter to the flora that battles to survive
in the windswept heights. Towering above the overhang loom the desolate peaks of
the Misty Mountains, like giant stone sentinels watching over the lands beneath
them.
name: A Dusty Mine Entrance

desc:
Three tunnels meet at an intersection that was once a busy distribution centre
for goods and tools going about in the mine. A stone ceiling curves gracefully
overhead, the craftsmanship of its constructor well-preserved over time. Even
the finely chiselled corners of the granite that meet in the centre are still
sharp and unspoiled. As the mine was abandoned years ago, the tunnel has now
been filled with dust and cobwebs, and each step taken whirls up the dust that
hangs silent in the air until caught in the webs.
name: Dusty Intersection

desc:
The dusty tunnel comes to a sudden stop on a ledge, looking down a fissure in
the stone. The smooth walls of the fissure are near vertical and very narrow,
making any climb very dangerous. It might be possible to find enough hand-holds
to descend, but there is no route up. The fissure narrows above, too thin for
even the smallest dwarf.
name: Before a Dark Tunnel

desc:
This tunnel, once crafted with skill and persistence by the Dwarves, is now
abandoned. Once the tunnel was swarming with busy miners transporting minerals
found on the lower levels. The air is thick with dust and cobwebs here, making
breathing tiresome and your throat dry. In a corner you see a heap of broken
tools and oily rags, presumably left when the mine was abandoned.
name: Dirty Tunnel

desc:
Cobwebs hang from the walls and ceiling, however even the small spiders that
once spun them have abandoned the mines. Piles of stone and ore are all around
as if the mine had been suddenly abandoned. The glint of valuable ore sticking
out through gashes in the walls beckons travellers to collect some of these
riches.
name: Dust-filled Chamber

desc:
The cracked dirt in the roof of this tunnel does not inspire confidence that it
will not collapse. However, taking into consideration that it has stood the test
of so much time already, it will most likely continue to bear the weight of the
earth above. Carved places in the walls show this mine to be still laden with
minerals. As with the connecting corridors, this place appears to be long
abandoned.
name: Cracked Dome

desc:
This nook has been undisturbed for many years. A few badly aged tools lay
propped against the wall. Should someone use a pick or shovel on the ancient
wooden supports, the twine that connects the wood to rock would no longer hold
the stone's weight. The wood itself is aged, but having once been well-carved it
still maintains its strength. The mine itself is still rich with ore, with small
side passages leading into the lode.
name: Dusty Corner

desc:
A tunnel hewed with great precision and skill runs through the dark mountain. It
was clearly devised, crafted and used by dwarves, to gain access to the deep
core of the mountain. The cold black stone on the floor is smooth, and would be
perfectly level if not for the marks rendered by the countless feet that once
worked in the mine. The tunnels have been abandoned for many years now, and a
layer of dust has settled on the surface of the stonework.
name: Dusty Mine Tunnel

desc:
One of many connecting the small villages of northern Dunland, this path is not
frequently trodden these days. The undergrowth on both sides has encroached on
it in parts, the thick overhanging bushes making movement tiring. To the west
and south the trail continues, winding its way through the plains.
name: Overgrown Trail

desc:
A worn path guides the way through high grass covering the vast plains. Small
rocks scattered alongside the path looks like they have been thrown aside to
make a clear passage on the trail. To the south are some small bushes growing
reluctantly in a shallow depression.
name: Worn Path

desc:
Wide open grasslands reach as far as the eye can see. The tough, green grass
bends gently in a light breeze. Some small bushes grow reluctantly in a small
depression to the north.
name: Open Grasslands

desc:
Flatlands covered with thick grass and small green bushes extend for miles in
every direction. Through the tough, green grass that waves lazily in the wind,
scattered rocks can be seen on the floor making travel difficult. The grasslands
continue to the north, while the plains open up in all other directions.
name: Vast Grasslands

desc:
A small cave excavated roughly from the stone has been made here to pen the
orkish mounts. It has lain uncleansed for many years, and every surface is now
utterly clad in filth. South a wooden gate to the pen swings on rusty hinges,
leading out into the main tunnel. The pen is built close to that entrance for a
good reason - so the orcs can flee speedily whenever the trolls come up from the
deeper caves for food.
name: A Filthy Pen

desc:
The crude and foul-smelling tunnel has been build recently and the many notches
and scars in the walls suggest the work was done by orcs. Little attention was
payed to decorations or finish; the construction was hurried and careless. The
tunnel contains no installations to make it suitable for living, yet a strong
smell of orcs, spiced with the unmistakeable stench of rotting meat, pervades
the air, sugesting the tunnel has other purposes. A hatchway made of bones, fur
and lack of skill covers an opening above the tunnel. West the smell of orcs
increases.
name: Primitive Tunnel

desc:
This roughly fashioned passageway has been cut without thought of direction or
ease of movement. Anyone taller than the average orc would have to stoop or risk
banging their head against the rock. The tunnel continues east, from which
direction a faint breeze of air comes, and south through a curtain of hanging
furs and skeletons. Both ways are too dark to see much further.
name: Badly-Cut Tunnel

desc:
A large cavern, excavated recently without much skill, extends north and east
here. Little attention has been payed to smoothening either the walls or the
floor, and the ceiling has many sharp edges that often hang dangerously low. A
dank and pressing stench penetrates the air, a natural and comfortable smell to
the inhabitants. Crude beds, made of tattered furs and branches, have been
clumsily arranged along the southern wall to serve as sleeping quarters for the
orcs living here.
name: Sleeping Quarters

desc:
Roughly hewn walls to the south and the east marks the end of a large cavern.
Excavated with an apparent lack of skill, the cavern would make a poor resort
even for a brief stay. Sharp edges and rocks in the floor don't promise a good
rest should you decide to make a stop here. This corner of the cavern appears to
have other use than resting; barrels and crates stacked against the eastern wall
together with a large alcove to the south makes the cavern a good spot to store
goods and loot. Unfortunately this collection of stores makes the cave a
frequent raiding point for trolls, who burrow tunnels through the rock to reach
it. Mostly these old tunnels have been collapsed or sealed up by the orcs, but
there is no way to stop newer tunnels being built.
name: Storage Area

desc:
Burrowing upwards the trolls found orkish caves above them, and have taken to
raiding them for food. Mostly the orcs find these tunnels and seal them off, but
this one they have not noticed. It has been dug in a winding fashion, up and
crossing a natural fissure in the rock. Above you it ends in a heavy but movable
slab of rock, while below it winds down to the fissure's base.
name: Burrowed Tunnel

desc:
This tunnel was originally the base of a huge natural fissure, which still
extends high above you. It was widened by the huge power of the cave trolls,
burrowing through the rock and earth with their bare hands. East and above you
the tunnel starts to wind upwards, while west it feeds into a large natural
cavern. At the moment this end of the tunnel is used by the troll bitches to
keep their young, and consequently it is extremely dirty.
name: Tunnel within a Fissure

desc:
Hidden in one of many caves that riddle the Misties, a small band of trolls
attempt to survive. The original cavern, naturally made, has been expanded with
tunnels running west and east of here, while huge thorn bushes have been dragged
over the entrance to obscure it. Most of the cave is rather filthy, the only
exception being a few fresh skins of bears and other large creatures.
name: Natural Cavern

desc:
In this small side cave the young trolls are kept, those that are too young to
kill food for themselves. It is a round cavern, with every surface caked in a
thick layer of dirt. Countless animal skeletons, from rabbit up to bear sized,
litter the cave, remnants of the vast meals these creatures need. East is the
main cavern, a high natural expanse much bigger than this burrowed one.
name: Filthy Cave

desc:
This east-west ridge rises sharply from the plains to the north, but stays
fairly level to the east and west. The highest point is about twenty-five feet
higher than the surrounding land. Small rocks can be seen poking out of the
ankle-length grass here and there. The Glanduin flows gently on to the south,
following the curves of the land
name: Ridge beside the Glanduin

desc:
This short and grassy hill rises gently from the east, creating a much wider and
taller ridge as the land travels to the west. The slope northwards is still
gentle, but less so. While a constant layer of grass covers the entire hill,
some portions of the southern slope have been claimed by patches of brush,
sheltered in part from the wind.
name: A Gentle Hill

desc:
The grass grows long underfoot, even though it is sparse in rockier areas.
Small, budding flowers are scattered throughout the area, thriving in the more
fertile areas of this gentle landscape. Scattered patches of exposed rock create
isolated brown islands in the vegetation. The land gradually slopes up to the
west, creating a hill in this otherwise flat land.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
A constant layer of hardy grasses lie on the ground, making a carpet of green.
The land is mostly flat, with only occasional gentle slopes to be seen. While
there are small shrubs dotting the landscape, they do little to add colour to
the area, or give shelter for travellers. Brownish rocks litter the ground at
the bottom of the slopes, creating little problems negotiating the area.
name: Wide, Grassy Plains

desc:
The land is flat, having no variation to its pitch or elevation. The only growth
that can be seen is a constant layer of grass, covering the land with a layer of
green. Small stones dot the ground, occasionally stacked into small piles by
some unknown entity. The air here is fresh, carrying the pure scent of the open
plains.
name: Open Plains

desc:
The ground here is rarely level, with mild slopes intersecting one another
constantly. The ground has a mixture of thick grasses and wild grains, creating
an ideal area for grazing. Many bushes line the creases created by two or more
slopes, often making it easier to walk around rather than through. Some fist-
sized rocks litter the ground, almost hidden by the surrounding vegetation.
name: In the Downs

desc:
Holes about a foot in diameter dot this area, littering the ground with small
depressions that are impossible to see without the observer being right beside
them. A traveller on foot would not have much trouble, but riders might find
their mounts stumbling. The grass is over a foot long, giving good grazing to
wild animals in the area.
name: Fertile Downs

desc:
Dips and rises in the surrounding plains make it easy to see for long distances,
but it can be difficult to determine exactly what will be on the other side of
an individual rise. Wild grasses and patches of flowers grow abundantly on every
available patch of ground. Not a stone or rock can be seen to mar the almost
total swath of vegetation.
name: In the Green Downs

desc:
Lush grass grows here, tall enough to cover a man's ankles. The land has a small
amount of tilt and curve to it, gradually rising as it travels to the south.
There are numerous small tracks and paths created mazelike in the grass, caused
by many rodent scurrying about their business. An odd flower has risen out of
the grass, creating a splash of colour to the green landscape.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
The ground here has a sporadic covering of vegetation. At times patches of grass
appear to be growing healthy and well, but in places only the toughest of weeds
will grow. The layer of earth covering this portion of plains appears to change
consistency often, leaving no vegetation in spots and a thriving mass in others.
Despite all of this, there is evidence that animals make their home here.
name: Grass-covered Plains

desc:
The land here is alive, though apparently not overly fertile. A fairly constant
covering of grass lies on the ground, neither thick nor overly tall. Many small
and hardy bushes eke out a living, clustered together in areas that the grass
does not grow in. Other portions of the plains would most likely offer more in
the way of hunting opportunities.
name: Wide, Grassy Plains

desc:
This area has little to see. A skimpy layer of earth allows only the hardiest
weeds and shrubs to grow, creating a landscape that is more brown than green.
Very few signs of animals life are apparent, mostly consisting of an old
skeleton lying on top of the ground. Its previous owner may have been a deer.
name: Desolate Plains

desc:
The ground underfoot is covered with a sparse layer of grasses, alive yet not
thriving on the open plains. The topsoil appears to be thin here, not allowing
the normal amount of plant life to flourish. Some long patches of flat rock
stick out of the earth, creating lifeless areas. Few animals seem to live here,
most likely preferring healthier portions of the plains.
name: Empty Plains

desc:
The ground here has a barely discernable slope, which rises upwards over half of
a mile to a small hill. Despite all of that, the hill is probably no more than
thirty feet above the surrounding plains. At its top sits a small cairn, worn by
age and the elements. Were it not for the otherwise featureless landscape, this
three-foot tall obstacle may have been difficult to spot.
name: Cairn in the Plains

desc:
The small amount of rise and fall the ground has here makes for a complacent
view, when mixed with the grass blowing in the wind. Thick and verdant grass
covers every inch of available soil, with wild flowers poking through in small
patches.
name: In the Downs

desc:
The land is made up of a series of small, grassy rises spaced unevenly apart.
None of those rises are large enough to be considered to be a hill, but make it
impossible to see for any distance unless the observer is standing atop one.
Grasses dominate the tops of the rises, with patches of flowers taking shelter
along the gentle slopes. The small valleys created have small shrubs in them
which are too small to impede any traveller's progress.
name: Fertile Green Downs

desc:
The ground is flat and covered in grass. In some places, the vegetation has been
trampled flat by many feet. There may be herds which use this area for pasture.
Many piles of dung lie in the trampled area, left by previous four- legged
visitors. Most of the tracks appear to come from and leave to the west, making a
broad path. There might be something of interest to a herd in that direction.
name: Green Fertile Pasture

desc:
The main focus of the land in this area seems to be a large pond which has most
likely seen cleaner days. Hoofprints have torn up all of the grass around it,
and manure covers much of the area. Despite all of that, the water appears to be
drinkable. The grass for hundreds of yards around the water has been stripped to
the ground, eaten by the animals which come to water here.
name: The Watering Hole

desc:
A healthy carpet of grass covers the ground, eaten short in spots by herbivores.
A rough animal track winds from the north to the east, making a round-about
turn. Away from the track, several small clusters of wild flowers grow. Dotting
the landscape are low-lying shrubs, vying for the sun in the wide open space.
name: Plains by the Watering Hole

desc:
A constant layer of grasses and tall weeds covers the ground for miles in every
direction. The land has little slope to it, being almost completely flat. A few
low-lying bushes can be seen, huddled close to the earth. The openness of this
area makes it ideal to see for miles in any direction, but offers little in the
way to see.
name: Far into the Vast Prairie

desc:
While generally flat, there are several long and gentle slopes, never steep
enough to cause dangerous footing. Healthy grass covers the ground for miles in
every direction. One or two small patches of flowers do little to add a splash
of colour to the environment, or rid the area of an air of monotony.
name: The Vast Prairie

desc:
The ground here is flat and notable landmarks are few and far between. While a
constant layer of grass covers the ground, it does not appear to flourish like
it does in the surrounding portions of the plains. Few splashes of colour mark
the landscape, making the whole area an almost uniform shade.
name: Vast Prairie

desc:
Small swells in the plains create a series of minute hills. The view from the
top of a swell lets a traveller see for over a mile in every direction, although
the dips often hide what they contain. A lush layer of grass thrives here,
flourishing in the fertile soil. Many wild flowers bloom alone or in small
patches, giving bright splashes of colour to the area.
name: Lush and Grassy Plains

desc:
The plains here are mostly flat, with small swells preventing a viewer from
seeing for miles in every direction. The wild grasses here grow several feet
tall, well fed by the fertile soil. Many aimless paths have been plowed through
the vegetation, most likely by the animals who come here to graze.
name: Tall Grass

desc:
This small room gives the impression of being eternally dark, the dark dirt
walls completely absorb light rather than reflecting it. The room is nothing
more than a rough seven-foot cube hollowed out from the surrounding earth. The
area is dry and the trapdoor above looks like it keeps out the rain well. The
remains of two long wooden boxes are the sole items of interest here.
name: In an Earthen Burial Mound

desc:
This peculiar-looking cave could be better described as a prolonged crack in the
face of the hill. The crack looks as if it may have been caused by a tremor that
rippled through the earth long ago, because it is very narrow with a high
ceiling. The crack ends in a smooth, rounded hemisphere, forming a cozy home for
a warg or, perhaps, a bear.
name: A Small Cave

desc:
Tall trees and dense thickets of shrubs and bushes create natural obstacles for
a traveller passing through the forest. At times the undergrowth become very
dense and an alternative route must be found around it. High above is a sea of
green treecrowns which blocks out most light and the sound of birdcalls can be
heard as your presence is announced throughout the forest.
name: Ancient Forest

desc:
The ancient forest consists of tall slender trees, many of them branchless far
up their straight stems, and a thick foliage of bushes and vines filling the
area between the trees. The undergrowth often reaches heights taller than a man
and the density of twigs and cuttings tangled together makes passage through the
forest far from a simple task. Far above are the tree-crowns, that has grown
into a dense entertwined carpet of dark-green leaves that leaves the forest cast
in permanent shadows.
name: Ancient Forest

desc:
Straight-stemmed trees, slender and knotless, support a green roof of leaves
that hover far above casting a cold shadow over the forest floor.. At the feet
of the trees grows corydalis and goutweed in tall beds encircling the tree
stems. The soft forest floor bounces beneath your feet, last years leaf-fall
lies thick still. Beneath the leaves is a fertile mould supporting the rich and
varied vegetation in the forest.
name: Ancient Forest

desc:
Towering trees with branchless stems stand majestic swaying in the wind. Dense
shrubbery and tangled bushes grow among the trees, and the brushwood shows no
inclination towards obeisance to a weary traveller passing through the forest.
Vines and sluggish branches seems to lunge at gear and limbs with their captive
thorns. East and south the undergrowth becomes so dense passage is impossible.
name: Ancient Forest

desc:
A long time ago someone must have excavated quite a large chamber within the
hill. Parts of the walls have collapsed now but wooden beams keep the whole
chamber from collapsing completely. Dust and earth sprinkle from the darkness
above, slowly covering you.
name: Earthy Crypt

desc:
Charred and blackened walls curve slightly forming a dome with the highest spot
a dozen feet above your head. The walls seem to have been made of stone and are
covered with soot in which someone has carved an intricate pattern. In the
middle of the chamber there is a brownish stoneblock with its upper parts
blackened with something unidentifiable.
name: Domed Chamber

desc:
A thinning grove of conifer trees borders the bank of a fast flowing brook.
Lined with algae and water lilies, during the summer months the water's edge
teems with insect life. To the east and west the stream continues. The trees
begin to fade to the south, where rolling plains pick up and dominate the
landscape, while a sheer side of the Misty Mountains looms to the north.
name: Babbling Brook

desc:
The calm river proceeds for a while along a small cliff to the west. Further
downstream the river turns to the west and out of sight behind the cliff. Under
the cliff runs a narrow and barren riverbank; on that narrow stretch of land
landing might be possible, but making a safe climb over the cliff could very
well prove impossible. Whereas the western bank is dreary and rocky there is
more comfort on the other side of the river. Bracken grows vigorously in the
moist soil, and behind them open fields stretch far into the horizon.
name: The River Glanduin

desc:
Old tiles that once supported the wall of this earthen tunnel have all but a few
loosened their grips and dratted into the water that now fills the tunnel. There
is little room to manoeuvre between the roots that penetrate the ceiling and the
water surface. The water is dark and cold, and besides a slight stir among the
vegetation that grows dense to the east, the water is stagnant. The tunnel
appears to be moving upwards to the west and slowly more room becomes available.
name: Flooded Tunnel

desc:
The tunnel runs east-west. While the water is steadily getting deeper to the
east, dry ground would most likely be found to the west where the condition of
the tunnel improves. Here the tiles in the walls are missing in some places and
the woodwork that supports the ceiling looks ready to collapse, rotten as it is.
Many different fungi have found the moist conditions suitable, and in places the
walls are covered from top to bottom by spores.
name: Wet Tunnel

desc:
Old walls of big stones laid as bricks form this dark tunnel. A ceiling of
wooden beams and planks appears reasonably intact apart from a few scratches and
imperfections near the northern wall. The air inside the tunnel is damp and
unpleasant to the throat, mainly due to spores originating from fungi that grow
frequently on the lower sections of the walls. Puddles of water on the floor lie
deceptively awaiting the next uncareful foot to wet.
name: Murky Tunnel

desc:
A fusty and moist smell fills the old cellar. The once fine stone walls have
felt the mark of time, and many cracks are visible as a result of damage by
frost. Thick oaken beams support a ceiling of half-rotten planks which have
collapsed in some places, resulting in fallen dirt. The previously spotless
paved stone floor is now a soggy mix of mud, dirt and puddles of water. The
remains of a system of wooden shelves, that once hosted a large collection of
wine, hang despondently from the iron nails that once fixed them to the walls. A
crumbling staircase, laid with bricks, stand against the northern wall.
name: Old Wine Cellar

desc:
On a cliff overlooking the river Glanduin to the east lie the ruins of the once
busy village of Cillien. Bricks and stones from the decaying masonry litter the
grass-covered ground between ruined stonewalls. Through time the stonework has
experienced such exposure to the elements, that it's no longer possible to
determine which walls were once part of the same structure. The remaining
stonework is partially covered in lichen and long vines that grow over the
cliffside down towards the river.
name: Ruins on a Cliff

desc:
A lazy upward slant continues to the south and east with the tip of the hill
some five or six miles away. An apple tree marks the landscape to the west and a
burned out hut is north of here. The land appears to be tranquil and signs of
elks are clearly visible.
name: Rising Hill

desc:
A cave-in has brought much dirt into the corridor and it has become necessary to
bend and crawl to pass through. The earthy environment is a combination of clay
and loose rubble through which many roots have found their way. With time the
damp air becomes pressing and longer periods of confinement in the cramped space
takes its toll on your endurance. A rubble-slide has sealed the corridor off to
the south, working your way through would appear to be hard work.
name: Caved-in Corridor

desc:
High upon the Misty Mountain you can see a narrow passage to the east and a deep
impassable crevice to the west. Often covered with snow, the wind blows without
mercy upon this peak making it difficult to remain here.
name: Mountain Ridge

desc:
With great effort you have managed to reach a narrow cleft high upon the
northern side of the mountain. Unfortunately, the cleft is coated in ice, has no
handholds and a strong stiff wind blows pushing you down the northern side some
five meters.
name: Misty Mountains

desc:
With great effort, you have managed to reach a narrow cleft high upon the
northern side of the mountain. Unfortunately, the cleft is coated in ice, has no
handholds and a strong stiff wind blows pushing you down the northern side some
eight meters.
name: Impassable Mountain

desc:
Trees, of mixed height and sort, mix and tangle their branches struggling for
space. A dense foliage of ever-green bushes take the remaining space at the feet
of the trees. The trees and the undergrowth together form an obstacle not easily
passed. Some feet above hovers the tree-crowns that are woven together by
twisting branches and dark leave; no light seems capable of passing through.
North the woodland seems to open up a little, a clearing or forest border must
be in that direction.
name: Ancient Forest

desc:
This wooden lift is no work of great craftsmanship. The maker of the lift can
not have had much skill with wood, and the well-worn planks forming the floor
have not been maintained in years. The sound of creaking beneath your weight is
not enough to cause alarm though, as the planks are made of oak strong and thick
enough to last. On the western and southern sides of the lift are openings in
the six foot fence through which to leave the lift. On its eastern side is a
small but noticeable metal socket.
name: Inside a Wooden Lift

desc:
High above the surrounding mountains you can see much of the landscape.
Unfortunately, you are unable to maintain your position on this peak and topple
down the west side; either gravely injured or more likely dead.
name: Impassable Mountain
